21: Elijah’s challenge to the assembled Israel — ʿad mātay ʾattem pōsᵉḥîm ʿal šᵉtê hassᵉʿippîm, ‘how long will you limp between two opinions (sᵉʿippîm)?’ — uses the precise vocabulary of structural division. The noun sᵉʿippîm is from sāʿap (to divide, fork) and means the branching forks of a tree: the image is of a person whose weight is distributed between two diverging branches, unable to commit to either. The verb pāsaḥ (to limp, to leap) is from the same root as pesaḥ (Passover): the verb means both to skip/leap and to limp — the double-forked stance produces an unstable gait. Psalms 119:113 uses the cognate: sᵉʿăpîm śānēʾtî — ‘I hate the double-minded,’ contrasting the undivided heart of the lover of Torah with the divided heart of the uncommitted. James 1:8 deploys the Greek equivalent: dipsychos (double-souled) — ‘a double-minded man is unstable in all his ways.’ James 4:8: ‘purify your hearts, you double-minded (dipsychoi).’ The OT text on which James draws is Elijah’s Carmel challenge. Matthew 6:24: ‘no one can serve two masters,’ and Revelation 3:15-16 (‘neither hot nor cold’) are the NT’s applications of the same structural point: the covenant demands undivided loyalty, and division between YHWH and any other absolute is the fundamental form of unfaithfulness.
38: The divine fire on Carmel — wayyippōl ʾēš YHWH wattōʾkal ʾet hāʿōlāh wᵉʾet hāʿēṣîm wᵉʾet hāʾăḇānîm wᵉʾet hāʿāpār wᵉʾet hammayim ʾăšer bātᵉʿālāh lāḥăkāh, ‘the fire of YHWH fell and consumed the burnt offering and the wood and the stones and the dust and licked up the water in the trench’ — is the theophanic fire of acceptance in its most dramatic form. The pattern is consistent across the OT’s inaugural sacrificial moments: Leviticus 9:24 marks the inauguration of the Levitical system (wattēṣēʾ ʾēš millipᵉnê YHWH wattōʾkal ʿal hammizbēaḥ, ‘fire came out from before YHWH and consumed on the altar’); 2 Chronicles 7:1 marks Solomon’s temple dedication (wāʾēš yārᵉḏāh min haššāmayim wattōʾkal hāʿōlāh wᵉhazzᵉḇāḥîm, ‘fire came down from heaven and consumed the burnt offerings and sacrifices’). The divine fire consuming the sacrifice signals YHWH’s acceptance and presence. Hebrews 12:29 carries the principle into the NT: kai gar ho theos hēmōn pyr katanaliskon, ‘for our God is a consuming fire.’ The Carmel fire is the crisis proof of what Hebrews 9:26 names as the final event in the same sequence: Christ ‘put away sin by the sacrifice of himself’ — the one, final, all-consuming sacrifice that makes all subsequent altar-fire unnecessary because it exhausted what the consuming fire always demanded.
1: bišnat haššlišit — in the third year. The drought oracle of 1 Kings 17:1 ('these years') is now temporally resolved: three years. The divine-word formula (wayᵉhî dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾel-ʾēlîyāhû) mirrors the commissions of 1 Kings 17, marking each phase of the confrontation as YHWH-initiated. Leḵ hirʾēh ʾel-ʾaḥāḇ — go, show yourself to Ahab. Hirʾēh (hithpael of rāʾāh) is 'present yourself' — Elijah is to make himself visible after three years of concealment at YHWH's command.
2: wayyēleḵ ʾēlîyāhû lᵉhirʾôṯ ʾel-ʾaḥāḇ — Elijah went to present himself to Ahab. Immediate obedience, same verb as 1 Kings 18:1. Wᵉhārāʿāḇ ḥāzāq bᵉšōmᵉrôn — the famine was severe in Samaria. Ḥāzāq (H2389, strong/intense) used of famine marks the crisis as maximum — the same adjective applied to the Egyptian drought-crisis (Genesis 47:20). The capital city Samaria is specified: the drought judgment has struck the seat of Ahab's power.
3: wayyiqrāʾ ʾaḥāḇ ʾel-ʿōḇadᵉyāhû ʾăšer ʿal-habbāyiṯ — Ahab summoned Obadiah, who was over the palace (steward, major-domo). ʿOḇadᵉyāhû means 'servant of YHWH' — the narrator immediately glosses his role: wᵉʿōḇadᵉyāhû hāyāh yārēʾ ʾeṯ-YHWH mᵉʾōḏ, Obadiah feared YHWH greatly. The name-and-character introduction creates the contrast: a man who fears YHWH serves in Ahab's court. The use of mᵉʾōḏ (greatly) marks him as an exemplary YHWH-fearer embedded in the apostate administration.
4: wayyihî biḥᵃkrôṯ ʾîzeḇel ʾeṯ-nᵉḇîʾê YHWH — when Jezebel was cutting off the prophets of YHWH. Biḥᵃkrôṯ (hiphil infinitive of kāraṯ, to cut off/eliminate) — the verb is used for breaking covenant, cutting off descendants, and killing; here the elimination of the prophetic class. Obadiah's response: wayyiqqaḥ ʿōḇadᵉyāhû mēʾāh nᵉḇîʾîm wayyaḥbîʾēm ḥamîššîm ḥamîššîm bammᵉʿārāh — he took 100 prophets and hid them fifty by fifty in a cave. Wayyᵉḵalkᵉlēm leḥem ûmāyim — he sustained them with food and water. The parallel to Elijah's own sustenance (ravens/widow) is structural: YHWH sustains his prophet supernaturally; Obadiah sustains the prophets through personal courage.
5: leḵ bāʾāreṣ ʾel-kol-maʿᵃyᵉnôṯ hammayim wᵉʾel-kol-hannaḥālîm — go through the land to every spring and every valley. Ahab's mission for Obadiah is purely pragmatic: find grass to keep the horses and mules alive so the royal cavalry does not perish. Pen-naḵrîṯ min-habbᵉhēmāh — lest we be cut off (kāraṯ) from livestock. The same kāraṯ root used for Jezebel's elimination of prophets (1 Kings 18:4) now describes the economic threat — verbal irony: the prophets are cut off; the horses must not be.
6: wayyaḥᵃlᵉqû lāhem ʾeṯ-hāʾāreṣ laʿăḇōr bāh — they divided the land between them to traverse it. Ahab went one way, Obadiah another — a logical search pattern. The division into two zones also narratively separates them so that Obadiah can encounter Elijah without Ahab's presence, creating the mediated meeting in 1 Kings 18:7–16.
7: wayyᵉhî ʿōḇadᵉyāhû baddereḵ wᵉhinnēh ʾēlîyāhû liqrāʾṯô — Obadiah was on the road, and behold, Elijah was coming to meet him. Hinnēh marks the surprising encounter. Wayyakkīrēhû wayyippōl ʿal-pānāyw — when Obadiah recognized him, he fell on his face. The prostration (nāpal ʿal-pānîm) before a prophet is an act of recognition of prophetic authority, not merely courtesy. Hāʾattāh zeh ʾăḏōnî ʾēlîyāhû — is it you, my lord Elijah? The address ʾăḏōnî (my lord) to a prophet is typical of prophetic deference.
8: wayyōʾmer lô ʾānî leḵ ʾĕmōr lăʾḏōneḵā hinnēh ʾēlîyāhû — Elijah answered: I am (here); go tell your master, 'Elijah is here.' ʾĀnî used as emphatic identification (cf. 17:1 Elijah's self-identification before Ahab). The command is simple and non-negotiable; Elijah does not offer to present himself directly but uses Obadiah as the intermediary, probably to prevent Ahab from having advance warning to prepare a military response.
9: mah ḥāṭāʾṯî kî-ʾattāh nōṯēn ʾeṯ-ʿaḇdᵉḵā bᵉyaḏ-ʾaḥāḇ lᵉhamîṯēnî — what have I sinned that you are giving your servant into Ahab's hand to kill me? Obadiah's protest is theologically framed: mah ḥāṭāʾṯî (what sin have I committed?) — the same formula used to protest unjust punishment. His fear is rational: Ahab has been searching for Elijah across every nation and kingdom (1 Kings 18:10); delivering the message risks the appearance that Obadiah has been hiding him.
10: ḥay YHWH ʾĕlōhayiḵā ʾim-yēš-gôy ûmamlāḵāh ʾăšer lōʾ-šālaḥ ʾăḏōnî šām lᵉḇaqšeḵā — as YHWH your God lives, there is no nation or kingdom where my master has not sent to search for you. The oath formula ḥay YHWH (as YHWH lives) here comes from Obadiah's mouth with the secondary form ʾĕlōhayiḵā (your God) — Obadiah uses Elijah's God as his witness. The universal search (every nation and kingdom) establishes Ahab's obsessive pursuit and the danger of Obadiah's position.
11: wᵉʿattāh ʾattāh ʾōmēr leḵ ʾĕmōr lăʾḏōneḵā hinnēh ʾēlîyāhû — and now you say, 'Go tell your master: Elijah is here.' The repetition of Elijah's command (1 Kings 18:8) in Obadiah's protest speech is a narrative device: Obadiah's three-part speech (1 Kings 18:9–14) circles back to the command each time, showing his genuine fear and the gravity of what is being asked. The repetition also maintains dramatic tension.
12: wᵉhāyāh ʾănî ʾēleḵ mēʾittāḵ wᵉrûaḥ YHWH yiśśāʾᵃḵā ʿal ʾăšer lōʾ-ʾēḏāʿ — as soon as I leave you, the Spirit of YHWH will carry you off (yiśśāʾ, from nāśāʾ, to lift/carry) to wherever I do not know. Obadiah fears Elijah will be miraculously transported (cf. 2 Kings 2:16 — the same phenomenon feared/expected for the prophet). Rûaḥ YHWH (Spirit/wind of YHWH) as the divine transport agent is consistent with prophetic transportation narratives. The consequence of Elijah's disappearance: Ahab will kill Obadiah for a false report.
13: hălōʾ-huggad laʾḏōnî ʾēṯ ʾăšer-ʿāśîṯî biḥᵃkrôṯ ʾîzeḇel ʾeṯ-nᵉḇîʾê YHWH — has my lord not been told what I did when Jezebel was killing the prophets of YHWH? Obadiah appeals to his track record: hiding (waʾaḥbîʾ) 100 prophets in caves and sustaining them. The appeal is both a defense of his YHWH-loyalty and an implicit argument for why Elijah should trust him. The narrative confirms 1 Kings 18:4 here — Obadiah himself testifies to his own action.
14: wᵉʿattāh ʾattāh ʾōmēr leḵ ʾĕmōr lăʾḏōneḵā hinnēh ʾēlîyāhû wᵉhārāgānî — and now you say, 'Go tell your master: Elijah is here,' and he will kill me. Third repetition of the command (cf. 1 Kings 18:8, 11) closes Obadiah's protest: the circularity of the speech mirrors his sense of entrapment. The conclusion wᵉhārāgānî (he will kill me) is the plain statement of his fear, without euphemism.
15: wayyōʾmer ʾēlîyāhû ḥay YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôṯ ʾăšer ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw — Elijah replied: as YHWH of hosts lives, before whom I stand. The oath formula here uses YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôṯ (LORD of hosts) — the divine warrior epithet linking YHWH to his heavenly armies, appropriate to the Carmel confrontation context. ʾĂšer ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw — before whom I stand — the prophetic standing-before (ʿāmad lipnê) is the technical idiom for royal/divine service (cf. 1 Kings 17:1). Elijah's counter-oath reassures Obadiah: the commission is real, and Elijah will appear today.
16: wayyēleḵ ʿōḇadᵉyāhû liqraʾṯ ʾaḥāḇ wayyagged-lô wayyēleḵ ʾaḥāḇ liqraʾṯ ʾēlîyāhû — Obadiah went to meet Ahab and told him, and Ahab went to meet Elijah. The brevity — no dialogue, no Ahab reaction to the news — accelerates the narrative to the direct confrontation. Obadiah is the mediator who successfully bridges the two encounters; having performed his function, he exits the narrative.
17: wayyihî kirʾôṯ ʾaḥāḇ ʾeṯ-ʾēlîyāhû wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw ʾaḥāḇ hăʾattāh zeh ʿōḵēr yiśrāʾēl — when Ahab saw Elijah, Ahab said to him, 'Is that you, you troubler of Israel?' ʿōḵēr (H5916, from ʿāḵar, to disturb/trouble) is the same root as the name ʿAḵān (Achan) — whose sin 'troubled' Israel in Joshua 7:25 (māh ʿăḵartānû YHWH yᵉʿāḵāreḵā). Ahab's accusation invokes this loaded term deliberately or by convention, placing prophetic disruption in the category of covenant-violation that brings corporate punishment.
18: wayyōʾmer lōʾ ʿāḵartî ʾeṯ-yiśrāʾēl kî ʾim-ʾattāh ûḇêṯ ʾāḇîḵā baʿăzōḇᵃḵem ʾeṯ-miṣwôṯ YHWH — he answered, I have not troubled Israel, but you and your father's house have, by abandoning (baʿăzōḇᵃḵem, from ʿāzaḇ) the commandments of YHWH. Elijah reverses the accusation with the same verb root and adds the specific charge: following the Baals (wattēleḵ ʾaḥărê habbᵉʿālîm). The counter-accusation is juridical: the true 'troubler' is the apostate king, not the prophet who calls for covenant fidelity.
19: wᵉʿattāh šᵉlaḥ qᵉḇōṣ ʾēlay ʾeṯ-kol-yiśrāʾēl ʾel-har hakkarmél — now send and gather all Israel to me at Mount Carmel. Carmel is on the northern border of the Jezreel valley, an ancient cultic high place. Elijah specifies two groups: the 450 prophets of Baal and the 400 prophets of Asherah (nᵉḇiʾê hāʾăšērāh) — totaling 850 prophets of the Jezebel court (ʾōḵᵉlê šulḥan ʾîzeḇel, eating at Jezebel's table — a patronage formula indicating royal support). The numerical asymmetry (850 vs. 1) frames the contest as impossible odds for Elijah.
20: wayyišlaḥ ʾaḥāḇ bᵉḵol-bᵉnê yiśrāʾēl wayyiqbōṣ ʾeṯ-hanᵉḇiʾîm ʾel-har hakkarmél — Ahab sent throughout all Israel and assembled the prophets at Mount Carmel. Ahab's compliance with the enemy prophet's command is noteworthy: either royal curiosity, implicit acknowledgment of Elijah's power, or an expectation that 850-to-1 odds would resolve the prophet problem. The narrative offers no interiority — Ahab's motive is left to the reader.
22: wayyōʾmer ʾēlîyāhû ʾel-hāʿām ʾănî nôṯartî nāḇîʾ laYHWH lᵉḇaddî ûnᵉḇiʾê habbāʿal ʾarbaʿ-mēʾôṯ waḥămiššîm ʾîš — I am the only remaining prophet of YHWH, while Baal's prophets number 450. Nôṯartî (niphal of yāṯar, to remain/be left over) — Elijah's self-description as the lone survivor is both accurate (Obadiah's 100 are hidden, not active) and rhetorically pointed: the disproportion is the theology. The Asherah prophets of 1 Kings 18:19 are absent from the contest — only the 450 Baal prophets participate.
23: yittᵉnû-lānû šᵉnayim pārîm — let them give us two bulls. Elijah proposes the symmetrical test: each side prepares a bull on wood without fire (ʾēš lōʾ yāśîmû), calls on their deity, and the god who answers by fire (hāʾĕlōhîm ʾăšer-yaʿăneh bāʾēš) is YHWH (hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm, he is God). The criteria is publicly agreed before any action — an adversarial-hearing structure that forecloses later dispute about the rules.
24: ûqrāʾtem bᵉšēm ʾĕlōhêḵem wăʾănî ʾeqrāʾ bᵉšēm YHWH — you call on the name of your god, and I will call on the name of YHWH. Calling on the name (qārāʾ bᵉšēm) is the standard worship-invocation formula in Hebrew (Genesis 4:26; 1 Kings 12:8). The parallelism is deliberate: same method, same structure — only the object differs. Wᵉhāyāh hāʾĕlōhîm ʾăšer-yaʿăneh bāʾēš hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm — the god who answers by fire, he is God. The people's acclamation: ṭôḇ haddāḇār, the thing is good.
25: wayyōʾmer ʾēlîyāhû lᵉnᵉḇiʾê habbāʿal bᵃḥărû lāḵem hāpar hāʾeḥāḏ — Elijah said to the Baal prophets, choose your bull first (hāriʾšōnāh) — you are the majority (kî ʾattem hārabbîm). The concession of first choice is simultaneously courteous and tactical: Baal's prophets receive no strategic disadvantage to excuse failure. The hours of calling that follow allow the audience to fully witness the non-response before Elijah's prayer.
26: wayyiqqᵃḥû ʾeṯ-hāpar ʾăšer-nāṯan lāhem wayyaʿăśû wayyiqrᵃʾû bᵉšēm-habbāʿal minhabbōqer ʿaḏ-haṣṣohŏrāyim lēʾmōr habbāʿal ʿānēnû — they called on the name of Baal from morning until noon, saying 'Baal, answer us!' Wayyᵉpasséḥû ʿal-hammizbēaḥ ʾăšer ʿāśāh — and they limped (pāsaḥ, H6452) around the altar they had made. The verb pāsaḥ links to the same word used in 18:21 for the people's wavering (pōsᵉḥîm on two branches) — the prophets' ritual limping dance uses the identical root, ironically embodying the Israel's spiritual wavering in physical form.
27: wayyihî baṣṣohŏrāyim wayyᵉhahēl bāhem ʾēlîyāhû wayyōʾmar qirʾû ḇᵉqôl-gāḏôl kî-ʾĕlōhîm hûʾ — at noon Elijah mocked them. Wayyᵉhahēl (hiphil of hāhēl, to mock/deride) is the language of ridicule. His taunt lists five possibilities for Baal's absence: śîaḥ (meditating/musing), śîg (busy/distracted — lit. turned aside), dereḵ lô (on a journey), biltî (perhaps, perhaps sleeping), yāšēn wᵉyîqāṣ (asleep and must awaken). The list is a theological demolition: a god who might be occupied, absent, or asleep cannot be YHWH, who neither slumbers nor sleeps (Psalms 121:4).
28: wayyiqrᵃʾû bᵉqôl gāḏôl wayyiṯgōḏᵉḏû kᵉmišpāṭām baḥᵃrāḇôṯ ûḇārᵉmāḥîm ʿaḏ-šᵉpāḵ-dām ʿălêhem — they shouted louder and cut themselves (wayyiṯgōḏᵉḏû, hithpael of gāḏad, to cut oneself) with swords and spears as was their custom, until blood flowed on them. Self-laceration as a cultic intensification rite is attested in Ugaritic and Mesopotamian religious practice; it was explicitly forbidden in Israel (Deuteronomy 14:1). The blood-letting is a desperate escalation that follows Elijah's mockery — Baal's prophets perform the most extreme cultic act they possess.
29: wayyihî kaʿăḇōr haṣṣohŏrāyim wayyiṯnabbᵃʾû ʿaḏ laʿălôṯ hamminḥāh wᵉʾên-qôl wᵉʾên-ʿōneh wᵉʾên qāšeḇ — midday passed, they raved (wayyiṯnabbᵃʾû, hithpael of nāḇāʾ, to prophesy/rave — here clearly ecstatic/frenzied behavior) until the evening offering, but there was no voice, no answer, no attention (qāšeḇ, H7182). The triple negative — ʾên-qôl wᵉʾên-ʿōneh wᵉʾên qāšeḇ — is an emphatic rhetorical device: Baal's total non-presence is marked at three levels (voice, response, regard). The 'evening offering' time (ʿălôṯ hamminḥāh) anchors the narrative in Israel's liturgical calendar, now repurposed as a countdown to Elijah's prayer.
30: wayyōʾmer ʾēlîyāhû lᵉḵol-hāʿām gᵉšû ʾēlay — Elijah said to all the people, come close to me. Gᵉšû (qal imperative plural of nāgaš, to draw near) invites communal witness. Wayyᵉrappēʾ ʾeṯ-mizbaḥ YHWH hehārûs — he repaired the broken (hehārûs, from hāras, to tear down/destroy) altar of YHWH. The altar's ruined state implies a pre-existing YHWH altar at Carmel that had been destroyed (by Jezebel's anti-YHWH campaign?). Repairing it is a symbolic act of restoration before the prayer.
31: wayyiqqaḥ ʾēlîyāhû šᵉtêm-ʿeśrēh ʾăḇānîm kᵉmispar šiḇṭê ḇᵉnê-yaʿăqōḇ — Elijah took twelve stones, according to the number of the tribes of Jacob's sons. The twelve-stone altar deliberately invokes the covenantal unity of all Israel (north and south) — this is not a Judean altar for Judah, nor a northern altar for the separated tribes, but an all-Israel confession. The name-change notice — ʾăšer hāyāh dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾēlāyw lēʾmōr yiśrāʾēl yihyeh šᵉmeḵā, to whom YHWH's word came saying 'Israel shall be your name' — invokes the Jabbok theophany of Gen 32, grounding the altar's twelve-tribe symbolism in the patriarchal covenant.
32: wayyiḇneh ʾeṯ-hāʾăḇānîm mizbēaḥ bᵉšēm YHWH — he built an altar in the name of YHWH. Building in šēm YHWH (the name of YHWH) is the formulation for legitimate, YHWH-authorized sanctuaries (cf. Deuteronomy 12:5; 1 Kings 5:17). Wayyaʿaś tᵉʿālāh kᵉḇêṯ sāʾtayim zeraʿ sāḇîḇ lammizbēaḥ — he dug a trench around the altar the size of two seahs of seed. The trench (tᵉʿālāh, H8585) is unusual — not a standard sacrificial feature; its purpose becomes clear when it fills with water (1 Kings 18:35), demonstrating the impossibility of natural ignition.
33: wayyiʿᵃrōḵ ʾeṯ-hāʿēṣîm wayyᵉnaṯṭēaḥ ʾeṯ-hāpar wayyāśem ʿal-hāʿēṣîm — he arranged the wood, cut the bull in pieces, and laid it on the wood. The preparation follows the standard altar-sacrifice procedure (Leviticus 1:6–8). Then: milʾû ʾarbaʿāh kaḏîm māyim wᵉyiṣqû ʿal-hāʿōlāh wᵉʿal-hāʿēṣîm — fill four jars of water and pour over the burnt offering and the wood. Water saturation of the altar deliberately eliminates any natural explanation for the fire that will follow.
34: wayyōʾmer šānû wayyišnû wayyōʾmer šallēšû wayyᵉšallēšû — he said, do it a second time, and they did; do it a third time, and they did. The triple pouring intensifies the impossibility: three applications, four jars each = twelve jars of water total (the number mirrors the twelve stones). The threefold pattern is also narratively emphatic — the scene is constructed to foreground the supernaturalness of what follows. The twelve-jar reading (4 × 3 pours) connects the water to the twelve-tribe symbolism of the twelve stones.
35: wayyēlᵉḵû hammayim sāḇîḇ lammizbēaḥ wᵉgam ʾeṯ-hattᵉʿālāh millēʾ māyim — the water ran around the altar and the trench was filled with water. This is the narrative verification that the saturation was complete: water filled the trench, the altar is soaked, wood is wet. The physical state is documented in detail because the subsequent fire's supernaturalness depends on the reader knowing exactly how impossible natural ignition would be.
36: wayyihî baʿălôṯ hamminḥāh wayyiggaš ʾēlîyāhû hannāḇîʾ wayyōʾmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾaḇrāhām yiṣḥāq wᵉyiśrāʾēl — at the time of the evening offering, Elijah the prophet drew near and said: YHWH, God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel. The three-part patriarchal address (ʾĕlōhê ʾaḇrāhām yiṣḥāq wᵉyiśrāʾēl) is the covenant identification formula — invoking the Abrahamic covenant in Carmel's context of covenant-renewal. The prayer is addressed to the one who made promises to the patriarchs. Hayyôm yiwwāḏaʿ kî ʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm bᵉyiśrāʾēl — let it be known today that you are God in Israel.
37: ʿānēnî YHWH ʿānēnî — answer me, YHWH, answer me. The double imperative (ʿānēnî repeated) is intensive prayer, not mere repetition. The stated purpose: wᵉyēḏᵉʿû hāʿām hazzeh kî-ʾattāh YHWH hāʾĕlōhîm wᵉʾattāh hăsibōṯāh ʾeṯ-libbām ʾăḥōranniṯ — that this people may know that you, YHWH, are God, and that you have turned their heart backward again. Elijah's prayer for the people's return (šûḇ implied in ʾăḥōranniṯ, backward/returning) is intercessory — the fire is the means, the people's repentance is the goal.
39: wayyarʾ kol-hāʿām wayyippᵉlû ʿal-pᵉnêhem wayyōʾmᵉrû YHWH hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm YHWH hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm — all the people saw and fell on their faces and said, 'YHWH, he is God; YHWH, he is God.' The acclamation (YHWH hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm) is the theological verdict of the contest: the people confess the monotheistic claim. The formula is repeated twice for emphasis — the covenant people re-acknowledge their covenant God. This acclamation echoes the contest's stated terms (1 Kings 18:24: 'the God who answers by fire, he is God') — the terms were met, the verdict follows.
40: wayyōʾmer ʾēlîyāhû ʾătᵉpᵉśû ʾeṯ-nᵉḇiʾê habbāʿal ʾîš ʾal-yimmālēṭ mēhem — Elijah said, seize the prophets of Baal — do not let one of them escape (ʾîš ʾal-yimmālēṭ). Wayyiṭbāḥēm šām ʾēlîyāhû bᵉnaḥal qîšôn — Elijah slaughtered them at the brook Kishon. The execution follows the logic of Deut 13: false prophets who lead Israel after other gods are to be put to death. The Kishon location connects to the Deborah-Barak victory over Sisera (Judg 4–5) — another site of divine intervention against Israel's oppressors.
41: wayyōʾmer ʾēlîyāhû lᵉʾaḥāḇ ʿᵃlēh ʾĕḵōl wᵉšᵉṯēh kî-qôl hămôn hāggāšem — Elijah said to Ahab, go up, eat and drink, for there is the sound of the rumbling of rain. Qôl hămôn hāggāšem — the sound of the abundance of rain — Elijah perceives prophetically what is not yet meteorologically present. The command to Ahab to eat and drink (normal activities) signals that the drought crisis is ending; Elijah speaks from prophetic certainty before any cloud appears.
42: wayyaʿal ʾaḥāḇ leʾĕḵōl wᵉlišṯôṯ wᵉʾēlîyāhû ʿālāh ʾel-rōʾš hakkarmél wayyiggaḥ ʾarṣāh wayyāśem pānāyw bên birkāyw — Ahab went up to eat and drink while Elijah climbed to the top of Carmel, crouched to the earth, and put his face between his knees. The posture of prayer — face between knees — is intense, concentrated supplication. Elijah's prayer posture here (unnamed but enacted) contrasts with his verbal prayer in 1 Kings 18:36–37; now he prays by position, watching through his servant.
43: wayyōʾmer ʾel-naʿărô ʿᵃlēh nāʾ habbeṭ dereḵ-yām — he said to his servant, go up now, look toward the sea. Seven times the servant goes and reports nothing — ʾên mᵉʾûmāh (there is nothing) — until the seventh report (1 Kings 18:44). The seven-fold watching pattern builds narrative tension; seven as a number of completion suggests the prophetic expectation was for the rain to come in its fullness, not prematurely. The servant as eyes for the praying prophet is consistent with the Elisha-Gehazi parallel in 2 Kgs 4.
44: wayyihî bašṣᵉḇîʿit wayyōʾmar hinnēh-ʿāḇ qᵉṭannāh kᵉkap-ʾîš ʿōlāh miyyām — at the seventh time he said, look, a small cloud like a man's hand rising from the sea. ʿĀḇ qᵉṭannāh kᵉkap-ʾîš — a little cloud, the size of a man's hand — the Hebrew kap is the palm or hand-span. The minimal beginning of the rain is the sign. Elijah's immediate command: hitch up (ʾesōr) and go down before the rain stops you. The prophetic urgency moves from patience (seven watches) to immediacy the moment the sign appears.
45: wayyihî ʿaḏ-kōh wᵉʿaḏ-kōh wᵉhaššāmayim qāḏᵉrû ʿāḇ wᵉrûaḥ wayyihî gesem gāḏôl — in a short time the sky grew dark with clouds and wind, and there was a great rain. ʿAḏ-kōh wᵉʿaḏ-kōh (until this and until this) is an idiom for a very short time — the transition from the tiny cloud to the great rain is nearly instantaneous. Wayyirkaḇ ʾaḥāḇ wayyēleḵ yizrᵉʿēʾl — Ahab rode and went to Jezreel. The narrative moves Ahab toward his capital city, setting up the next episode.
46: wayyihî yad-YHWH ʾel-ʾēlîyāhû wayyᵉšannēs mōṯnāyw wayyārāṣ lipnê ʾaḥāḇ ʿaḏ-bōʾᵃḵā yizrᵉʿēʾlāh — the hand of YHWH was on Elijah (yad-YHWH ʾel), he tucked up his loins (wayyᵉšannēs mōṯnāyw), and ran before Ahab to the entrance of Jezreel. Yad-YHWH (the hand of YHWH) is the standard idiom for divine empowerment of a prophet for an extraordinary act (Ezekiel 1:3; 1 Kings 3:22; 1 Kings 8:1). The run to Jezreel — approximately 18 miles through a storm — is presented as a divine-power feat. Elijah runs before Ahab's chariot, which is at once an act of royal honor (the prophet precedes the king) and a demonstration of prophetic authority over the political order.
